channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Second Jury Finds Uber Responsible for Sexual Assault by a Driver
item.description: The ride-hailing giant has now lost the first two of more than 3,000 pending federal lawsuits.
item.dc:creator: Emily Steel
item.media:credit: Joseph Seif for The New York Times
item.media:description: Uber faces scrutiny across the country as lawmakers, investors and others move to hold the company accountable for sexual violence during rides.

item.title: Elon Musk Ignores French Prosecutors, Widening Tech Rift With Europe
item.description: Prosecutors investigating his social media company, X, had summoned him for a meeting. His no-show reflected a broader dispute over regulation.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:description: Elon Musk speaking in Switzerland in January. He has previously denounced the French investigation.

item.title: Kevin Warsh, Trump’s Fed Pick, to Defend Bank’s Ability to Set Rates Independently
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh is set to testify at the Senate Banking Committee on Tuesday even as a criminal investigation into the central bank risks delaying his ascension to become the next chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh is President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve.

item.title: The Onion Signs New Deal to Take Over Infowars
item.description: A new deal, which would allow The Onion to use the Infowars name and website address, must be approved by a Texas judge.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Elizabeth Williamson
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Onion, a satirical news outlet, wants to convert the right-wing Infowars site into a parody of itself.

item.title: The Peace Rally Gets Derailed
item.description: Oil prices are climbing and global stocks are falling as investors worry about the prospects for a speedy end to the war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Tensions around the Strait of Hormuz have again put hopes for a quick end to the war in the Middle East in doubt.

item.title: The President Is Coming to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: A president who relishes attacking the news media is set to break his boycott of an event celebrating the news media. (The first lady is attending, too.) What could go wrong?
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: President Trump has sued ABC News, CBS News, The Wall Street Journal and The New York Times, among other news organizations since the run-up to the 2024 election.

item.title: Trump Administration Takes Steps to Refund $166 Billion in Tariffs
item.description: The government debuted a system to repay importers two months after the Supreme Court struck down tariffs at the heart of the president’s trade policy.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump opposed tariff refunds until the Court of International Trade intervened in March and ordered the government to return the money to importers.

item.title: Why Gas Prices Go Up Fast and Take So Long to Fall
item.description: Fuel station owners take some of the hit for consumers when oil prices surge. On the way down, they try to get their money back.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Simon Simard for The New York Times
item.media:description: Oil prices climbed in recent weeks to the highest level since 2022. Gas station owners don’t like price spikes any more than their customers do.

item.title: The Night the Government Closed the Skies Over El Paso
item.description: A high-energy laser weapon and a power struggle between federal agencies brought a night of hassles to the city.
item.dc:creator: Kate Kelly, Tawnell D. Hobbs, Reyes Mata III and Paul Ratje
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: The airspace around El Paso International Airport, including a roughly 11-mile diameter area from Sunland Park, N.M., to Horizon City, Texas, was temporarily closed in February for what the Federal Aviation Administration called “special security reasons.”

item.title: C.E.O.s Are the Heads of Companies. Should They Also Be the Face?
item.description: Most chief executives are not recognizable to their customers. But when they step into the limelight, the rewards — and the risks — can be great.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Burger King’s chief executive, Tom Curtis, at the company’s Miami headquarters. He not only appeared in a recent commercial but also invited customers to call him.

item.title: Javier Milei Tamed Argentina’s Inflation. Now He Wants to Reshape Its Values.
item.description: Argentina’s right-wing president has tamed the country’s runaway inflation. Now he wants to transform its values.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Sarah Pabst
item.media:description: Javier Milei in Mar del Plata, Argentina, in January at the Derecha Fest, a large right-wing gathering.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump and Stocks Waver on Renewed Iran Conflict
item.description: Traders, hopeful on Friday that a peace deal was near, were digesting the developments over the weekend.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: My Boss Loves ChatGPT. Must I Fake Loving It Too?
item.description: Plus, if you’re feeling old and sidelined at work, this is the fictional character to channel.
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Luxury Brands Bet on the Middle East. War Has Damaged Their Plans.
item.description: High-end brands such as Louis Vuitton and Hermès are looking to other regions as sales in Persian Gulf nations plummet.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton SA
item.media:description: A Louis Vuitton store in Paris. LVMH has seen a drop in sales in its Middle Eastern stores, with demand falling across the gulf region.

item.title: Americans Living Abroad for Lower Costs Now Say Returning Home Is Too Expensive
item.description: Americans have enjoyed lifestyles that would normally be out of reach to them by working remotely in countries with lower living costs.
item.dc:creator: Will McCurdy
item.media:credit: Tako Robakidze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nino Trentinella has built a new life in Tbilisi, Georgia, where she can afford a housekeeper and regular meals at restaurants.

item.title: Are Democrats Becoming a Party of Tax Cuts?
item.description: As Democrats try to find a way back to power in Washington, some see tax cuts as a quick and easy way to address affordability. The wonks are freaking out.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren

item.title: This Is a Hard Time to Start a Career. These Two Words Can Help.
item.description: Advice on building a rewarding work life, even amid employment gloom.
item.dc:creator: Jodi Kantor
item.category: How to Start: Discovering Your Life's Work (Book)

item.title: Syrian Billionaires Needed a Favor in Washington. They Invoked the Trump Name.
item.description: The attempt by the Khayyats to influence foreign policy while discussions are underway about potential Trump family deals is an increasingly common feature of the president’s second term.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Laura Boushnak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Moutaz and Ramez Al-Khayyat, two of three Syrian-born brothers, at their company’s headquarters in Doha, Qatar.

item.title: Humans Who Used a Bear Suit to Defraud Car Insurers Sentenced to Jail
item.description: The California residents collected more than $141,000 in insurance payouts after staging bear attacks on their luxury cars, state officials said.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: A California Department of Fish and Wildlife biologist who reviewed video footage of the attacks on luxury cars said that the animal was “clearly a human in a bear suit,” the California Department of Insurance said.

item.title: Man Charged in Lego Theft Scheme of Replacing Pieces With Pasta, Police Say
item.description: A California man was charged with grand theft after the police said he reaped about $34,000 in what an official called an “off the charts” pasta-and-switch scheme involving Lego kits.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: The police said a California man bought Lego sets to orchestrate a refund scheme that involved bags of pasta.

item.title: Hollywood Talent Giant Draws Suitors Amid Epstein Fallout
item.description: Formerly known as Wasserman, the agency that has changed its name to The Team put itself up for sale after its founder’s emails surfaced in the Epstein files.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Casey Wasserman last year. He announced in February that he would be exploring a sale of the talent agency he founded.

item.title: Why Your Paycheck Feels Smaller
item.description: Ben Casselman, our chief economics correspondent, explains why wages are not keeping up with inflation and what that means for American workers and the economy.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman, Nour Idriss, Sutton Raphael and Stephanie Swart

item.title: Corporate Profits Are at Record Highs. These 4 Factors Could Sink Them.
item.description: Experts have different theories about what’s driving steep gains. But many agree the window for growth is shrinking.
item.dc:creator: Peter Coy
item.media:description: An Amazon data center being built in New Carlisle, Ind.

item.title: Reopening Strait of Hormuz Would Ease Oil Crisis but Only So Much
item.description: Analysts said energy and shipping companies would be reluctant to fully restore operations until they were confident that hostilities were over.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil tanker off Basra, Iraq, on Friday. If the Strait of Hormuz were to fully open, it would take weeks for substantial amounts of Persian Gulf oil and gas to reach buyers around the world.

item.title: More Americans Taking 401(k) Hardship Withdrawals
item.description: More Americans are taking hardship withdrawals from workplace retirement accounts, prompted by rising costs — and looser rules.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Kristen Zeis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Rad had to cover several unexpected expenses last year, including dental surgery for her 10-year-old dachshund, Dewey.

item.title: The Mighty Engine That Jaguar Built, and Built Jaguar
item.description: For 42 years, the XK was Jaguar’s secret weapon. It proved its worth on the racetrack and powered generations of cars. The company’s leap into electrics could take a similar path.
item.dc:creator: Paul Stenquist
item.media:description: Vintage Jaguars in Mollis, Switzerland.

item.title: Federal Court Temporarily Freezes Nexstar’s Merger With Tegna
item.description: The judge said the two television companies could not combine operations while an antitrust lawsuit proceeded. Nexstar said its deal was already done.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:description: A federal judge temporarily blocked Nexstar from combining its operations with the station group Tegna.

item.title: American Airlines Says It Won’t Merge With United
item.description: American issued the statement after news reports that United Airlines’ chief executive had discussed the idea with the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: The U.S. airline industry is dominated by four large carriers, and any deals between them would face significant scrutiny from lawmakers and consumers.

item.title: Mississippi Liquor Stores Run Dry Amid State Distribution Delays
item.description: The state liquor agency has been struggling to make timely deliveries to restaurants, bars and liquor stores since it got rid of outdated conveyor belts at a warehouse in January.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Bare shelves at Spillway Wine and Spirits in Brandon, Miss., on Thursday. Technical issues with the replacement of an aging conveyor system have contributed to prolonged delays in liquor deliveries by the state.

item.title: Sazerac Prepares $15 Billion Cash Offer for Maker of Jack Daniel’s
item.description: The Kentucky spirits company’s bid for Brown-Forman comes as the industry is being buffeted by a decline in alcohol consumption.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:description: The potential all-cash bid for Brown-Forman, the maker of Jack Daniel’s, comes as it is already engaged in talks to be acquired by Pernod Ricard of France.

item.title: Kevin Klose, Who Made NPR a Reporting Powerhouse, Dies at 85
item.description: A longtime journalist at The Washington Post, he used his unexpected fund-raising talents to greatly expand the radio organization’s coverage.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Kevin Klose in 2003, when he was president and chief executive of NPR. By 2005, he had boosted the network’s average weekly audience by 50 percent, to 26 million.

item.title: Mark Mobius, Pioneering Investor in Emerging Markets, Dies at 89
item.description: Gaining a reputation as the brilliant, risk-taking “Indiana Jones” of his field, he encouraged investors to take chances on Asia, Africa, Latin America and Eastern Europe.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: Franklin Templeton Limited Duration Income Trust
item.media:credit: K.Y. Cheng/South China Morning Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mark Mobius in 2001. Working for Franklin Templeton in the late 1980s, he started one of the first investment funds anywhere dedicated to emerging markets.

item.title: Trump Seeks More Time in Lawsuit Against IRS Over His Tax Returns
item.description: The Justice Department has not responded to the president’s suit, which has created a conflict of interest for the government’s lawyers.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: Lawyers for President Trump asked a judge to extend a key deadline in his lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service.

item.title: Hundreds of Fake Pro-Trump Avatars Emerge on Social Media
item.description: The artificial-intelligence-generated fake influencers have surged on TikTok, Instagram, Facebook and YouTube in an apparent bid to hook conservative voters.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu

item.title: Stocks Extend Rally as Tensions Ease Over War in Iran
item.description: The S&P 500’s rise on Friday caps a striking three-week streak, powered by investors’ optimism about the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and strong corporate earnings.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Oil Prices Fall Sharply After Iran Says Strait of Hormuz Is Open
item.description: But analysts said it was not clear how quickly the oil industry in the Persian Gulf would be able to get back to normal.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Is Open During Cease-Fire in Lebanon, U.S. and Iran Say
item.description: Statements from President Trump and Iran aimed to raise confidence in the safety of the waterway, but shipping experts said risks remained.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Jenny Gross and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo ships line up in the Strait of Hormuz as seen from the United Arab Emirates in March.

item.title: You Have Some Options for Dealing With Rising Property Taxes
item.description: The average bill rose 3 percent last year, in part because of higher tax rates. There are ways to try to reduce what you owe, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Washington’s Scramble to Get Mythos, Anthropic’s Powerful New Model
item.description: Federal agencies have requested access to Claude Mythos Preview, which Anthropic says can rapidly identify — and potentially create — new cyberthreats.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Government agencies are seeking access to Anthropic’s most powerful artificial intelligence model, Claude Mythos Preview.

item.title: The Only Thing N.B.A. Players Love More Than Basketball Is Starting Podcasts
item.description: Bench players and stars alike see fun and career possibilities behind the mic.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Podcasting is “a great opportunity for us for possibly life after basketball,” said Jalen Brunson, a co-host of “Roommates Show.”

item.title: Ellison Pledges at Least 30 Paramount-Warner Movies a Year
item.description: His remarks at a movie theater convention were part of a campaign to win Hollywood support for his bid to buy Warner Bros. Discovery.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:description: David Ellison, the chief executive of Paramount Skydance, speaking at CinemaCon on Thursday.

item.title: What Heelys, the Sneakers With Wheels, Taught Me About Momentum as a Child
item.description: Heelys, the sneakers with wheels that were a fad in the 2000s, helped me form my first friendships. Recently, I decided to tap into that joy again.
item.dc:creator: Ash Wu
item.media:description: Ash Wu wearing a pair of Heelys. Reconnecting with the shoes brought back memories of her childhood.

item.title: Roger Adams Dies at 71; Invented the Rolling Sneakers Known as Heelys
item.description: You could walk in them like gym shoes, but if you rocked back on your heels the wheels emerged, turning them into roller skates. In the early 2000s, the company sold millions.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Politics News Site NOTUS to Become ‘The Star’
item.description: The Washington publication is rebranding as it expands its local news and sports coverage in the wake of substantial layoffs at The Washington Post.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Ting Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Allbritton, the founder of NOTUS and a former owner of Politico, will fund The Star’s expansion.

item.title: Despite Cease-Fire, Iran’s Hackers Haven’t Logged Off
item.description: Tehran’s digital warriors have continued to seek ways to gain an advantage in the conflict in a new phase of cyberspace operations.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators in Tehran this week. Iran is trying to keep up pressure on the United States and Israel with cyberattacks but also positioning itself to mount a bigger retaliation if peace talks do not resume.

item.title: Pentagon Seeks Help From Ford and G.M.
item.description: Concerned about the slow pace and high cost of weapons production, Pentagon officials have begun talks with General Motors and Ford Motor about producing certain parts.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman, Neal E. Boudette and John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Pentagon has met with Ford Motor and General Motors to gauge whether the auto industry may be able to help the military acquire vehicles, munitions and other hardware.

item.title: Reed Hastings Will Leave Netflix as Board Chairman
item.description: The co-founder of the streaming giant will leave its board in June, the company said.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Alex Goodlett for The New York Times
item.media:description: “My real contribution at Netflix wasn’t a single decision; it was a focus on member joy,” the Netflix co-founder and chairman Reed Hastings said in a letter about his coming departure.

item.title: NPR Receives $113 Million From 2 Gifts
item.description: The donations, from the philanthropist Connie Ballmer and an anonymous donor, will support the network’s long-term strategy.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: NPR studios in Washington, DC.

item.title: Condé Nast Shutters Self Magazine
item.description: The publishing giant is also closing the international editions of Glamour Magazine in Germany, Spain and Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Ilya S. Savenok/Getty Images for SELF Magazine
item.media:description: Self, the health and fitness publication, has been available only digitally since 2017.

item.title: Despite Iran War’s Effects, PepsiCo Says Consumers Still Spending on Snacks
item.description: Executives at the food and drink giant announced a rise in sales of drinks and snacks but warned that “inflation will come.”
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: PepsiCo’s revenue rose 8.5 percent in the first quarter of the year, to $19.4 billion.
